meta.name: SSOSH Self-Stigma of Seeking Help Scale
meta.description: The SSOSH is a 10-item scale measuring self-stigma associated with seeking psychological help. It assesses the degree to which individuals perceive that seeking professional help would reduce their self-regard. Higher scores indicate greater self-stigma, which may serve as a barrier to help-seeking behavior.
meta.author: Vogel et al. (2006)
meta.reference: Vogel DL, Wade NG, Haake S. Measuring the self-stigma associated with seeking psychological help. J Couns Psychol. 2006;53(3):325-337.
meta.instruction: People at times find that they face problems that they consider seeking help for. This can bring up reactions about what seeking help would mean. Please indicate your degree of agreement or disagreement with each statement.

questions.text: I would feel inadequate if I went to a therapist for psychological help
My self-confidence would NOT be threatened if I sought professional help
Seeking psychological help would make me feel less intelligent
My self-esteem would increase if I talked to a therapist about my problems
My view of myself would not change just because I made an appointment with a therapist
It would make me feel inferior to ask a therapist for help
I would feel okay about myself if I made the decision to seek professional help
If I went to a therapist, I would be less satisfied with myself
My self-confidence would remain the same if I sought help for a problem I could not solve
I would feel worse about myself if I could not solve my own problems

ranges.label: Low Self-Stigma
ranges.description: You have low self-stigma associated with seeking psychological help. You are unlikely to view help-seeking as a threat to your self-image.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your open attitude toward seeking help
Your willingness to seek help when needed is a valuable resource
You can serve as a positive role model for others who may be hesitant about seeking help

ranges.label: Mild Self-Stigma
ranges.description: You have mild self-stigma about seeking psychological help. While generally open, you may sometimes feel slightly uncomfortable about the idea of professional help.
suggestions: Recognize that seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness
Consider that most people benefit from professional support at some point in life
Challenge any self-critical thoughts about needing help
Learn more about the therapeutic process to reduce uncertainty

ranges.label: Moderate Self-Stigma
ranges.description: You have moderate self-stigma about seeking help. Concerns about self-image may significantly influence your willingness to seek professional psychological support.
suggestions: Reflect on where these beliefs about help-seeking come from
Consider that seeking help demonstrates self-awareness and courage
Talk to trusted friends or family members about their experiences with therapy
If you are experiencing psychological distress, the benefits of seeking help far outweigh any perceived stigma

ranges.label: High Self-Stigma
ranges.description: You have high self-stigma about seeking psychological help. Strong concerns about self-image may serve as a significant barrier to getting the support you need.
suggestions: Recognize that high self-stigma can prevent you from accessing beneficial support
Many successful and capable people seek and benefit from therapy
Consider exploring anonymous or online counseling as a first step
Challenge the belief that needing help means you are inadequate
If you are struggling, please consider reaching out despite these feelings
